sections.heading: Availability Zones and Supported Regions
sections.body:
Atlas supports the following Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions and
[availability zones](microsoft-azure-availability-zones).
While all of the following regions support dedicated clusters (`M10+`),
some regions don't support Free clusters (formerly known as `M0`)
or Flex clusters.
The Atlas API uses the corresponding
**Atlas Region**.

> **Note:** Clusters spun up in an Atlas organization created through Azure (Microsoft Azure) Native
> Integration aren't limited to the specific Azure (Microsoft Azure) region where the organization
> is based. Clusters can be deployed in any of the 40+ worldwide Azure (Microsoft Azure)
> regions in which Atlas is supported. The organization region is only required
> to fit the Azure (Microsoft Azure) resource requirements and has no impact on the regions where
> clusters in the organization are deployed.

> **Important:** We recommend that you use the regions marked by an asterisk
> (asterisk) in the following table as a secondary disaster
> recovery (DR) region only in a [multi-region cluster](manual/reference/glossary) because
> these regions cost higher than the other regions in the table.
>
> Also, these regions might not be available in your Azure (Microsoft Azure) environments
> without approval from Azure (Microsoft Azure) support. If you want to leverage private
> networking options, such as [VPC/VNet peering](vpc-peering) or
> [private endpoint connections](private-endpoint), with a
> cluster deployed in one or more of these regions, you must allow
> your Azure (Microsoft Azure) subscription to create resources in these regions. To
> learn more, contact [Azure support](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/support/).

Americas

Europe

Asia Pacific

Africa

Middle East and Africa

Atlas supports [NVMe storage volumes](nvme-storage) and [Extend Storage Capacity and IOPS on Azure](change-storage-capacity-azure) in the following Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions.

Americas

Europe

Asia Pacific

Middle East and Africa

sections.heading: Cluster Configuration Options
sections.body:
Each Atlas cluster tier comes with a default set of resources.
Atlas provides the following resource configuration options:

Custom Storage Size

The size of the server root volume. Atlas clusters deployed onto
Azure (Microsoft Azure) use premium SSD (Solid State Disk)s.
[^azure-disk-docs]

> **Note:** 
>
> RAM Availability
>
> The actual amount of RAM available to each cluster tier might be
> slightly less than the stated amount, due to memory that the kernel
> reserves.

> **Note:** As of October 18, 2021, the following Atlas clusters deployed to
> Azure (Microsoft Azure) offer 16,000 IOPS (up
> from 7,500) and 500 MB/second throughput (up from 250 MB/second):
>
> - New clusters with 4 TB storage volumes.
>
> - Existing clusters that you scale up to 4 TB storage volumes.

The following cluster tiers are available:

| Cluster Tiers | Storage Range  | Default Storage | Default RAM |
| ------------- | -------------- | --------------- | ----------- |
| M0            | .5 GB          | .5 GB           | Shared      |
| Flex          | 5 GB           | 5 GB            | Flex        |
| M10           | 8 GB to 128 GB | 8 GB            | 2 GB        |
| M20           | 8 GB to 256 GB | 16 GB           | 4 GB        |
| M30           | 8 GB to 512 GB | 32 GB           | 8 GB        |
| M40           | 8 GB to 1 TB   | 64 GB           | 16 GB       |
| M40 Low-CPU   | 8 GB to 1 TB   | 128 GB          | 16 GB       |
| M50           | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 128 GB          | 32 GB       |
| M50 Low-CPU   | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 128 GB          | 32 GB       |
| M60           | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 128 GB          | 64 GB       |
| M60\_NVME     | 1600 GB        | 1600 GB         | 64 GB       |
| M60 Low-CPU   | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 128 GB          | 64 GB       |
| M80           | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 256 GB          | 128 GB      |
| M80 Low-CPU   | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 256 GB          | 128 GB      |
| M80\_NVME     | 1600 GB        | 1600 GB         | 128 GB      |
| M200          | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 256 GB          | 256 GB      |
| R200          | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 256 GB          | 256 GB      |
| M200\_NVME    | 3100 GB        | 3100 GB         | 256 GB      |
| R300          | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 512 GB          | 384 GB      |
| M300\_NVME    | 3600 GB        | 3600 GB         | 384 GB      |
| R400          | 8 GB to 4 TB   | 512 GB          | 432 GB      |
| M400\_NVME    | 4000 GB        | 4000 GB         | 512 GB      |
| M600\_NVME    | 4000 GB        | 4000 GB         | 640 GB      |

 Can use this tier for a multi-cloud cluster.

 Not available in the following regions:

- **germanywestcentral**

- **switzerlandnorth**

- **switzerlandwest**

> **Note:** 
>
> Cluster Tier & API Naming Conventions
>
> For purposes of management with the [Atlas Administration API](atlas-api), cluster tier names that are prepended with `R`
> instead of an `M` (`R40` for example) run a [class](storage-class-ui) version of the cluster. When
> [creating](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupcluster) or
> [modifying](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupcluster) a
> cluster with the API, be sure to specify your desired cluster class
> by name with the
> `replicationSpecs[n].regionConfigs[m].electableSpecs.instanceSize`
> attribute.

> **Important:** 
>
> Multi-Cloud Low-CPU clusters
>
> Low-CPU cluster tiers (M40 Low-CPU, M50 Low-CPU, M60 Low-CPU, and so
> on) are available in [Configure High Availability and Workload Isolation](create-cluster-multi-region) as long as the cluster tier is
> available for all the regions that the cluster uses.

Workloads typically require less than `2TB`.

[NVMe storage volumes](nvme-storage) don't support
[Configure High Availability and Workload Isolation](create-cluster-multi-region).

Atlas configures the following resources automatically and does not allow
user modification:

Encrypted Storage Volumes

Azure (Microsoft Azure) storage volumes are
[always encrypted](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/azure-managed-disks-sse/).

sections.heading: Azure Availability Zones
sections.body:
Azure (Microsoft Azure) maintains multiple data centers within each
region. Azure (Microsoft Azure) groups the data centers into [availability zones](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/availability-zones/az-overview),
which are separate locations within the region. Maintaining data centers in
different physical locations helps Azure (Microsoft Azure) tolerate local failures.

Azure (Microsoft Azure) availability zones aren't available in all
regions. To learn which Azure (Microsoft Azure)
regions maintain availability zones, see the [Azure](microsoft-azure-supported-regions).
In regions where availability zones aren't yet available, Azure (Microsoft Azure) uses
[fault domains](microsoft-azure-fault-domains) to help ensure failure
tolerance.

Atlas uses Azure (Microsoft Azure) availability zones automatically
when you deploy a dedicated cluster to a region that supports them. Atlas splits the cluster's nodes across
availability zones. For
example, a three-node replica set cluster would have one node deployed onto
each zone. A local failure in the Azure (Microsoft Azure) data center hosting
one node doesn't impact the operation of data centers
hosting the other nodes.

> **Note:** Regions with availability zones provide higher
> uptime for dedicated clusters deployed after September 12, 2019.
> Clusters deployed before September 13, 2019 to regions that now offer availability
> zones aren't split across availability zones automatically. To learn more about availability
> zones, see Azure (Microsoft Azure)'s [documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/availability-zones/az-overview).

sections.heading: Azure Fault Domains
sections.body:
Each Azure (Microsoft Azure) region
includes a set number of fault domains for failure tolerance. Fault domains consist of a group of
virtual machines that share a common power source and network switch. If you deploy your cluster
to a region that doesn't support availability zones, Atlas spreads the nodes across the
fault domains instead.

Atlas uses [availability sets](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/availability-set-overview)
to deploy clusters across fault domains. For regions that have at least three
fault domains (3FD), Atlas deploys clusters across three fault domains. For regions
that only have two fault domains (2FD), Atlas deploys clusters across two fault domains.

The Atlas Add New Cluster form marks regions
that support 3FD (Three Fault Domain) clusters as
**Recommended**, as they provide higher availability.

The number of fault domains in a region has no effect on the number of MongoDB
nodes Atlas can deploy. MongoDB Atlas clusters are always made of
replica sets with a minimum of three MongoDB nodes.

For general information on Azure (Microsoft Azure) fault domains and availability sets, see
[Availability Sets Overview](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/availability-set-overview)

sections.heading: Regions with at Least Three Fault Domains
sections.body:
If the selected Azure (Microsoft Azure) region has at least three
fault domains, Atlas clusters are split across three fault domains. For
example, a three node replica set cluster would have one node deployed onto
each zone.

![A 3-node replica set deployed across a 3-fault-domain Azure region.](/reference/images/microsoft-azure-3az.svg)

3FD (Three Fault Domain) clusters have higher availability
compared to 2FD (Two Fault Domain) clusters. However, not all
regions support 3FD (Three Fault Domain) clusters.

sections.heading: Regions with Only Two Fault Domains
sections.body:
If the selected Azure (Microsoft Azure) region has two fault domains,
Atlas clusters are split across the two fault domains. For example, a
three node replica set cluster would have two nodes deployed to one zone and
the remaining node deployed to the other zone.

![A 3-node replica set deployed across a 2-fault-domain Azure region.](/reference/images/microsoft-azure-2az.svg)

2FD (Two Fault Domain) clusters have a higher chance of loss of
availability in the event of the loss of an zone than 3FD
(Three Fault Domain) clusters. However, where latency or location are a
priority, a region that supports 2FD (Two Fault Domain) clusters
may be preferred.

[^azure-disk-docs]: For detailed documentation on Azure (Microsoft Azure) storage
    options, see [High-performance Premium Storage and managed disks for VMs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/storage-premium-storage)

sections.heading: Supported Regions
sections.body:
Atlas supports deploying separate [Search Nodes](configure-search-nodes) for `M10` and higher
clusters on Azure (Microsoft Azure).

Atlas doesn't support Search Nodes in the following Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions:

| Region Name                                            | Azure Region       |

| ITALY\_NORTH (available for low-CPU Search Nodes only) | italynorth         |

| NORWAY\_WEST                                           | norwaywest         |
| SOUTH\_AFRICA\_WEST                                    | southafricawest    |

| US\_NORTH\_CENTRAL                                     | northcentralus     |

sections.heading: Available Search Tiers
sections.body:
Atlas provides different [Search Tier](select-tiers-for-search-nodes) for Search Nodes
deployed on Azure (Microsoft Azure).

The following table lists the search tiers available for each class of
hardware (low-CPU and high-CPU), with default RAM and storage measured
in gibibytes (GiB):

Low-CPU

High-CPU

sections.heading: Stream Processing Workspaces
sections.body:
> **Important:** To request an unlisted region, add your feedback
> to the [MongoDB Feedback Portal](https://feedback.mongodb.com/?category=7529546585152608284). Your
> input helps prioritize future expansions of Atlas Stream Processing coverage.

Atlas supports deploying [Stream Processing Workspaces](atlas-sp-manage-spi) in the follow Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions:

Americas

Europe

sections.heading: Integrations
sections.body:
Along with [Microsoft Azure](microsoft-azure), the following
product integrations enable applications running on Azure (Microsoft Azure), such as
Azure Virtual Machines, Azure Functions, and Azure Container Instances,
to use Atlas instances easily and securely.

sections.heading: Networking Services
sections.body:
- Azure Virtual Network: [VPC/VNet peering](vpc-peering)

- Azure Private Link: [private endpoint connections](private-endpoint)

- Azure Key Vault:

  - [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption)

  - [Configure client-side field level encryption with Key Vault](manual/core/security-client-side-encryption-key-management)

sections.heading: Security and Identity Services
sections.body:
- Microsoft Entra ID: Configure federated authentication to the MongoDB UI

- Microsoft Entra ID Domain Services: Configure database user authentication and authorization

sections.heading: Procurement
sections.body: - Azure Marketplace: [Pay for Atlas usage through Azure](https://azuremarketplace.microsoft.com/en-us/marketplace/apps/mongodb.mongodb_atlas_self_serve_prod_2022?tab=Overview)

sections.heading: Integrations with other Azure Services
sections.body:
- Azure Databricks: [Read and write to Atlas using Databricks and Apache Spark](https://docs.databricks.com/data/data-sources/mongodb.html)

- Azure Data Factory: [Copy data from or to MongoDB Atlas using Azure Data Factory or Synapse Analytics](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/data-factory/connector-mongodb-atlas)

- Azure Native Integration: [Create Atlas organizations from Azure, billed self-service](azure)

sections.heading: More Information
sections.body:
For more information on how to use Azure (Microsoft Azure) with Atlas most effectively,
review the following best practices, guides, and case studies:

- Power BI Desktop: Connect to Atlas from Power BI Desktop

- Visual Studio (VS) Code: [Work with MongoDB from VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/azure/mongodb)
